# C++ Kernel Experimental Branch — Discovery Report

**Source branch:** `claude/cpp-core-experiment-cEsyH`
**Generated by:** Claude Code (Opus 4.7), session `rswOF` (Discovery branch `claude/discover-cpp-kernel-rswOF`)
**Generated on:** 2026-05-07
**Repository:** `Crystalka228/Dual-Frontier`

> **Read me first.** Two facts dominate everything below and are easy to miss otherwise:
>
> 1. **The branch and `main` share no common ancestor.** `git merge-base main claude/cpp-core-experiment-cEsyH` returns nothing — these are disjoint git histories. The branch was clearly imported from a parallel repo line, not branched off of `main`. As a result, anything written below as "diff vs main" is a **tree comparison**, not a topological diff. Words like "MODIFIED" or "DELETED" mean "differs between the two trees", not "the branch changed it on top of main".
> 2. **The C++ kernel work is just 11 commits at the top of a 151-commit branch.** The 140 underlying commits are an older snapshot of the same project that is largely already covered, in newer form, on `main`. The actual experimental delta — the part that matters for integration — is small and well-bounded.
>
> Both points are crucial to Section 11 (integration analysis). Read them before drawing conclusions.

### Branch was paused mid-sentence

The branch's last commit on 2026-04-24 (`e2bc2d9`) is not a closure — it is a fix to BenchmarkDotNet DLL loading. There is no decision-recorded commit afterwards. The companion document `docs/NATIVE_CORE.md` §6 ("Decision rule") still has the line:

> Results will be filled in after BenchmarkDotNet is run on a real workstation (the probe sandbox does not have a .NET SDK available).

…even though the BenchmarkDotNet results **were** subsequently produced (see §10). The decision matrix in §6 has not been re-applied to the actual numbers; it is still stuck in "awaiting workstation run" state.

## Section 2 — Commit narrative

### 2.1 The C++ experiment slice (11 commits)

Reading the branch from its earliest C++-relevant commit (`7b5cf78`, 2026-04-24) to HEAD (`e2bc2d9`, same day) — **the entire experiment landed in a single working day**:

```
e2bc2d9 experiment: fix native DLL loading in BenchmarkDotNet --full mode
f59492a Add native build files, VSCode config, benchmarks
cdeedbb docs: add development hygiene checklist
dd5de10 docs: fix engine/game boundary for post-release fork
cb65761 docs: add Native Core Experiment document
959ccd0 docs: document native core experiment approach and results
80178c2 experiment: add NativeVsManaged benchmark
6eac732 experiment: add DualFrontier.Core.Interop with P/Invoke wrappers
cf0eed3 experiment: implement native World with C API
a8d235e experiment: implement SparseSet template in C++
7b5cf78 experiment: scaffold native core directory and CMakeLists
```

### 2.2 Themed grouping

**Phase A — Foundation (`7b5cf78`)**
CMakeLists, directory layout. No code yet.

**Phase B — Algorithm port (`a8d235e`, `cf0eed3`)**
`SparseSet<T>` (template) and `RawComponentStore` (type-erased) come up first — these are the data structures. Then `World` (entity lifecycle + deferred destroy) lands and the C API surface is plumbed through `capi.cpp`.

**Phase C — C# bridge (`6eac732`)**
`DualFrontier.Core.Interop` project: P/Invoke declarations, `NativeWorld` managed handle, `EntityIdPacking`, FNV-1a `NativeComponentType<T>`.

**Phase D — Benchmark harness (`80178c2`)**
`DualFrontier.Core.Benchmarks` — both the BenchmarkDotNet job and the Stopwatch smoke runner. `BenchHealthComponent` introduced as a struct sibling of the production class-based `HealthComponent` so the two worlds can be compared apples-to-apples.

**Phase E — Documentation (`959ccd0`, `cb65761`, `dd5de10`, `cdeedbb`)**
- `docs/NATIVE_CORE.md` — English, focuses on architecture boundary, P/Invoke mapping, marshalling options, decision rule.
- Substantial revisions to `docs/NATIVE_CORE_EXPERIMENT.md` — Russian, focuses on motivation, decision criterion (revised away from "20% faster mean" toward p99/GC-pause/long-run drift on weak hardware).
- `docs/methodology/DEVELOPMENT_HYGIENE.md` — added; engine/game boundary checklist that pre-emptively names `interop:` and `native:` as legitimate scope prefixes.

**Phase F — Build/run wiring (`f59492a`, `e2bc2d9`)**
- `f59492a` adds `.vscode/settings.json`, `CppProperties.json`, the BenchmarkDotNet artifact directory (yes, results are committed — see Section 10), and a copy of the built `DualFrontier.Core.Native.dll` next to the benchmark project.
- `e2bc2d9` (the final commit) fixes a BenchmarkDotNet `--full` DllNotFoundException by adding a `<None Include=...>` content item with a build-time `Condition="Exists(...)"` guard so the project still loads when the native library has not yet been compiled.

### 2.3 Notable commits

| Hash | Why notable |
|---|---|
| `7b5cf78` | First C++ commit. Sets the directory layout (`native/DualFrontier.Core.Native/{include,src,test}`) and locks in C++20 + MSVC/GCC/Clang via CMake 3.20. |
| `a8d235e` | First real algorithm port. The SparseSet template proves the layout choice (sparse / dense / dense-to-index) survives translation 1:1 with the managed `ComponentStore.cs`. |
| `cf0eed3` | World + C API. The single most consequential commit — defines the entire P/Invoke surface (12 functions). |
| `6eac732` | Bridge crosses. Once this commit is in, the managed and native worlds can interoperate. |
| `80178c2` | First measurement infrastructure. Benchmark introduced; `BenchHealthComponent` (struct) introduced as the workaround for the class-based production catalog. |
| `e2bc2d9` | Final commit. Fix for a real-world failure (BenchmarkDotNet generates a child project under temp; the native DLL has to ride along as a content item). The fact that this fix was needed proves the BenchmarkDotNet path was actually exercised. |

### 2.4 No "struggle" commits

There are no `revert`, `fix(experiment): undo X`, or `try Y instead` commits. The C++ slice reads as a confident first-draft single-day pass — scaffold, ports, bridge, harness, docs, ship. Possible interpretations:

- The author had a clear plan and executed it (likely given the structured doc set).
- The author stopped exactly when the first non-trivial obstacle (decision-rule application against actual numbers, marshalling for class-based components) became visible.

The branch metadata cannot disambiguate these — both fit the evidence. The presence of completed BenchmarkDotNet results that the doc still describes as "to be filled in" suggests the author landed the numbers, then walked away from the decision step.

## Section 3 — File tree changes vs main

### 3.1 Caveat — disjoint histories

Because main and the branch share no common ancestor, the diff is a tree-vs-tree comparison. A file shown as `D` (deleted) is simply present in `main` but absent from the branch — it does not mean the branch deleted it. Likewise `M` is "differs", not "branch modified". This makes the global numbers (601 files, ±60k lines) noise relative to the question we actually care about.

## Section 4 — Build configuration

### 4.1 Inferred toolchain

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Build system | CMake ≥ 3.20 |
| Languages | CXX (C++20, no extensions, `CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD_REQUIRED ON`) |
| Default build type | `Release` (forced if not set, with `-O3` on GCC/Clang or `/O2` on MSVC) |
| Compilers supported | GNU, Clang, AppleClang, MSVC |
| Compiler flags (GNU/Clang) | `-Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic -fvisibility=hidden` |
| Compiler flags (MSVC) | `/W4 /permissive-` |
| Output kind | `SHARED` library + `df_native_selftest` executable |
| Output name | `DualFrontier.Core.Native` (with `PREFIX ""` so Linux/macOS produce `…Native.so` / `…Native.dylib` not `lib…`) |
| Position-independent code | `ON` |
| Build-defining macro | `DF_NATIVE_BUILDING_DLL` (private, used in `df_capi.h` to flip `__declspec(dllexport)` ↔ `__declspec(dllimport)`) |
| External dependencies | None — pure standard library (`<vector>`, `<unordered_map>`, `<memory>`, `<cstdint>`, `<cstring>`, `<chrono>`, `<cstdio>`) |

### 4.2 `native/DualFrontier.Core.Native/CMakeLists.txt`

```cmake
cmake_minimum_required(VERSION 3.20)
project(DualFrontier.Core.Native LANGUAGES CXX)

set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD 20)
set(CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD_REQUIRED ON)
set(CMAKE_CXX_EXTENSIONS OFF)

if(NOT CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE)
    set(CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE Release CACHE STRING "Build type" FORCE)
endif()

if(CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER_ID MATCHES "GNU|Clang|AppleClang")
    add_compile_options(-Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic -fvisibility=hidden)
    add_compile_options($<$<CONFIG:Release>:-O3>)
elseif(MSVC)
    add_compile_options(/W4 /permissive-)
    add_compile_options($<$<CONFIG:Release>:/O2>)
endif()

set(DF_NATIVE_SOURCES
    src/world.cpp
    src/component_store.cpp
    src/capi.cpp
)

set(DF_NATIVE_HEADERS
    include/df_capi.h
    include/sparse_set.h
    include/component_store.h
    include/world.h
    include/entity_id.h
)

add_library(DualFrontier.Core.Native SHARED ${DF_NATIVE_SOURCES} ${DF_NATIVE_HEADERS})

target_include_directories(DualFrontier.Core.Native PUBLIC
    $<BUILD_INTERFACE:${CMAKE_CURRENT_SOURCE_DIR}/include>
)

target_compile_definitions(DualFrontier.Core.Native PRIVATE DF_NATIVE_BUILDING_DLL)

set_target_properties(DualFrontier.Core.Native PROPERTIES
    OUTPUT_NAME "DualFrontier.Core.Native"
    PREFIX ""
    POSITION_INDEPENDENT_CODE ON
)

option(DF_NATIVE_BUILD_SELFTEST "Build the native self-test executable" ON)
if(DF_NATIVE_BUILD_SELFTEST)
    add_executable(df_native_selftest test/selftest.cpp)
    target_link_libraries(df_native_selftest PRIVATE DualFrontier.Core.Native)
    target_include_directories(df_native_selftest PRIVATE include)
endif()
```

### 4.3 `native/DualFrontier.Core.Native/build.md`

```markdown
# DualFrontier.Core.Native — Build Instructions

This is the experimental C++ core. It is **not** part of the `.sln`; CMake
builds it independently and the resulting shared library is copied next to the
C# assembly that hosts the P/Invoke wrappers (`DualFrontier.Core.Interop`).

## Output artifacts

| Platform | File name |
|----------|-----------|
| Windows  | `DualFrontier.Core.Native.dll` |
| Linux    | `libDualFrontier.Core.Native.so` — rename to `DualFrontier.Core.Native.so` or set `DllImport(Name)` accordingly |
| macOS    | `libDualFrontier.Core.Native.dylib` |

The CMake target sets `PREFIX ""` so on Windows the output is exactly
`DualFrontier.Core.Native.dll`. On Linux/macOS the usual `lib` prefix is
suppressed; the default `SUFFIX` (`.so` / `.dylib`) is retained.

## Windows (Visual Studio 2022)

cmake -S . -B build -A x64
cmake --build build --config Release
# Artifact: build\Release\DualFrontier.Core.Native.dll

## Linux / WSL

cmake -S . -B build -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release
cmake --build build -j
# Artifact: build/DualFrontier.Core.Native.so

## macOS

cmake -S . -B build -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release
cmake --build build -j
# Artifact: build/DualFrontier.Core.Native.dylib

## Self-test

The build produces a standalone executable `df_native_selftest` that
exercises the full C ABI (CRUD, deferred destruction, swap-with-last remove,
throughput). Run it after every build to validate the library before the C#
benchmark picks it up:

./build/df_native_selftest
# -> ALL PASSED

## Copying the artifact to the C# project

The C# `DualFrontier.Core.Interop` project expects the native library to sit
next to its own assembly at runtime. Post-build copy example (PowerShell):

Copy-Item native\DualFrontier.Core.Native\build\Release\DualFrontier.Core.Native.dll `
         src\DualFrontier.Core.Interop\bin\Release\net8.0\

Or automate it via an `AfterBuild` target inside
`DualFrontier.Core.Interop.csproj` once the native build path is stable — this
wasn't added to the csproj because the experiment keeps the two build systems
decoupled while feasibility is being assessed.
```

### 4.4 BenchmarkDotNet csproj (only mechanism that bundles the native DLL into a managed build)

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
    <AssemblyName>DualFrontier.Core.Benchmarks</AssemblyName>
    <RootNamespace>DualFrontier.Core.Benchmarks</RootNamespace>
    <IsPackable>false</IsPackable>
    <GenerateDocumentationFile>false</GenerateDocumentationFile>
    <AllowUnsafeBlocks>true</AllowUnsafeBlocks>
  </PropertyGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageReference Include="BenchmarkDotNet" Version="0.13.12" />
  </ItemGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <ProjectReference Include="..\..\src\DualFrontier.Contracts\DualFrontier.Contracts.csproj" />
    <ProjectReference Include="..\..\src\DualFrontier.Core\DualFrontier.Core.csproj" />
    <ProjectReference Include="..\..\src\DualFrontier.Core.Interop\DualFrontier.Core.Interop.csproj" />
  </ItemGroup>
  <!-- BenchmarkDotNet spawns each benchmark in a child process inside a generated
       project under a temp directory and copies our build output into it. The
       native library is not a managed dependency, so it has to ride along as a
       regular content file or the child process gets DllNotFoundException. -->
  <ItemGroup>
    <None Include="$(MSBuildThisFileDirectory)..\..\native\DualFrontier.Core.Native\build\Release\DualFrontier.Core.Native.dll"
          Condition="Exists('$(MSBuildThisFileDirectory)..\..\native\DualFrontier.Core.Native\build\Release\DualFrontier.Core.Native.dll')">
      <CopyToOutputDirectory>PreserveNewest</CopyToOutputDirectory>
      <Link>DualFrontier.Core.Native.dll</Link>
    </None>
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>
```

This is the only file that knows the native DLL has to ride along into a managed build output. There is no `runtimes/{rid}/native/...` layout and no NuGet packaging — see §13 risks.

All files are full-included below.

### 5.2 `include/df_capi.h` — public C ABI

```cpp
#ifndef DF_CAPI_H
#define DF_CAPI_H

#include <stdint.h>

#if defined(_WIN32)
    #if defined(DF_NATIVE_BUILDING_DLL)
        #define DF_API __declspec(dllexport)
    #else
        #define DF_API __declspec(dllimport)
    #endif
#else
    #define DF_API __attribute__((visibility("default")))
#endif

#ifdef __cplusplus
extern "C" {
#endif

/*
 * DualFrontier.Core.Native — flat C ABI for P/Invoke.
 *
 * Entity encoding: an EntityId is packed into uint64_t as
 *     high 32 bits = Version (signed int, reinterpreted as uint32_t)
 *     low  32 bits = Index   (signed int, reinterpreted as uint32_t)
 * This matches the C# EntityId(Index, Version) record struct layout when
 * marshalled as a single 64-bit value.
 *
 * Component storage: the native world is type-erased. The C# side chooses a
 * stable uint32_t type_id for each component type (e.g. a hash of the CLR
 * type's full name) and declares the component's byte size. The native side
 * copies `size` bytes on Add and on Get. For the PoC we assume blittable
 * structs; reference-type components remain on the C# side and are not
 * supported by this API (see docs/NATIVE_CORE.md).
 *
 * Return codes:
 *   0 — failure / not found
 *   1 — success / present
 * Out-of-range inputs return 0 rather than crashing.
 */

typedef void* df_world_handle;

DF_API df_world_handle df_world_create(void);
DF_API void            df_world_destroy(df_world_handle world);

DF_API uint64_t        df_world_create_entity(df_world_handle world);
DF_API void            df_world_destroy_entity(df_world_handle world, uint64_t entity);
DF_API int32_t         df_world_is_alive(df_world_handle world, uint64_t entity);
DF_API int32_t         df_world_entity_count(df_world_handle world);
DF_API void            df_world_flush_destroyed(df_world_handle world);

DF_API void            df_world_add_component(
                           df_world_handle world,
                           uint64_t entity,
                           uint32_t type_id,
                           const void* data,
                           int32_t size);

DF_API int32_t         df_world_has_component(
                           df_world_handle world,
                           uint64_t entity,
                           uint32_t type_id);

DF_API int32_t         df_world_get_component(
                           df_world_handle world,
                           uint64_t entity,
                           uint32_t type_id,
                           void* out_data,
                           int32_t size);

DF_API void            df_world_remove_component(
                           df_world_handle world,
                           uint64_t entity,
                           uint32_t type_id);

DF_API int32_t         df_world_component_count(
                           df_world_handle world,
                           uint32_t type_id);

#ifdef __cplusplus
}
#endif

#endif /* DF_CAPI_H */
```

### 5.3 `include/entity_id.h`

```cpp
#pragma once

#include <cstdint>

namespace dualfrontier {

// Mirror of the C# EntityId(Index, Version) record struct.
// Stored on the native side as two 32-bit values; packed to uint64_t at the
// C ABI boundary.
struct EntityId {
    int32_t index = 0;
    int32_t version = 0;

    [[nodiscard]] bool is_valid() const noexcept {
        return index > 0 || version > 0;
    }
};

inline uint64_t pack_entity(EntityId id) noexcept {
    const uint64_t lo = static_cast<uint32_t>(id.index);
    const uint64_t hi = static_cast<uint32_t>(id.version);
    return (hi << 32) | lo;
}

inline EntityId unpack_entity(uint64_t packed) noexcept {
    EntityId id;
    id.index = static_cast<int32_t>(packed & 0xFFFFFFFFu);
    id.version = static_cast<int32_t>((packed >> 32) & 0xFFFFFFFFu);
    return id;
}

} // namespace dualfrontier
```

### 5.4 `include/sparse_set.h`

```cpp
#pragma once

#include <cstddef>
#include <cstdint>
#include <cstring>
#include <stdexcept>
#include <vector>

namespace dualfrontier {

// Header-only SparseSet template.
//
// Mirrors the algorithm in src/DualFrontier.Core/ECS/ComponentStore.cs:
//   * _sparse[entity_index] -> dense slot, or -1 if absent.
//   * _dense packs live values contiguously for cache-friendly iteration.
//   * _dense_to_index maps dense slot back to entity index, needed for the
//     swap-with-last erase.
//
// Intentionally trivial: no allocator customisation, no exception safety
// beyond strong guarantee on std::vector growth, no thread safety — the
// calling layer (World) serialises access. This keeps the hot path aligned
// with the managed version being benchmarked against.
template <typename T>
class SparseSet {
public:
    static constexpr int32_t kAbsent = -1;

    SparseSet() = default;

    void add(int32_t entity_index, const T& value) {
        if (entity_index <= 0) {
            throw std::invalid_argument("SparseSet: entity_index must be > 0");
        }
        ensure_sparse_capacity(entity_index);

        int32_t dense_index = sparse_[entity_index];
        if (dense_index != kAbsent) {
            dense_[dense_index] = value;
            return;
        }

        const int32_t new_dense_index = static_cast<int32_t>(dense_.size());
        dense_.push_back(value);
        dense_to_index_.push_back(entity_index);
        sparse_[entity_index] = new_dense_index;
    }

    void remove(int32_t entity_index) noexcept {
        if (entity_index <= 0 ||
            entity_index >= static_cast<int32_t>(sparse_.size())) {
            return;
        }
        const int32_t dense_index_to_remove = sparse_[entity_index];
        if (dense_index_to_remove == kAbsent) {
            return;
        }

        const int32_t last_dense_index =
            static_cast<int32_t>(dense_.size()) - 1;

        if (dense_index_to_remove != last_dense_index) {
            const int32_t last_entity_index = dense_to_index_[last_dense_index];
            dense_[dense_index_to_remove] = dense_[last_dense_index];
            dense_to_index_[dense_index_to_remove] = last_entity_index;
            sparse_[last_entity_index] = dense_index_to_remove;
        }

        dense_.pop_back();
        dense_to_index_.pop_back();
        sparse_[entity_index] = kAbsent;
    }

    [[nodiscard]] bool has(int32_t entity_index) const noexcept {
        if (entity_index <= 0 ||
            entity_index >= static_cast<int32_t>(sparse_.size())) {
            return false;
        }
        return sparse_[entity_index] != kAbsent;
    }

    // Returns pointer into the dense array — valid until the next mutation.
    [[nodiscard]] T* get_ptr(int32_t entity_index) noexcept {
        if (!has(entity_index)) return nullptr;
        return &dense_[sparse_[entity_index]];
    }

    [[nodiscard]] const T* get_ptr(int32_t entity_index) const noexcept {
        if (!has(entity_index)) return nullptr;
        return &dense_[sparse_[entity_index]];
    }

    [[nodiscard]] int32_t count() const noexcept {
        return static_cast<int32_t>(dense_.size());
    }

    [[nodiscard]] const std::vector<int32_t>& dense_indices() const noexcept {
        return dense_to_index_;
    }

    [[nodiscard]] const std::vector<T>& dense() const noexcept {
        return dense_;
    }

private:
    void ensure_sparse_capacity(int32_t entity_index) {
        if (entity_index < static_cast<int32_t>(sparse_.size())) return;
        const std::size_t new_size =
            std::max<std::size_t>(
                static_cast<std::size_t>(entity_index) + 1,
                sparse_.empty() ? 256 : sparse_.size() * 2);
        sparse_.resize(new_size, kAbsent);
    }

    std::vector<int32_t> sparse_;
    std::vector<T> dense_;
    std::vector<int32_t> dense_to_index_;
};

} // namespace dualfrontier
```

> Observation. `SparseSet<T>` is **never instantiated** anywhere in the shipped library — `RawComponentStore` does not use it (see §5.5). It is built but unused. It exists as a strict translation of `ComponentStore.cs`'s sparse-set algorithm and is presumably kept around to demonstrate the template path or to be wired into a typed-store future variant. The C ABI does not need it.

### 5.5 `include/component_store.h` — type-erased store (the real workhorse)

```cpp
#pragma once

#include <cstdint>
#include <cstring>
#include <memory>
#include <stdexcept>
#include <vector>

#include "sparse_set.h"

namespace dualfrontier {

// Type-erased component store used by the C ABI.
//
// Storage is a SparseSet<RawSlot> where RawSlot wraps `size` bytes of opaque
// component data. The component type id (uint32_t) is assigned by the C#
// caller; the native side only tracks the byte size declared on first Add
// for that type.
//
// For the PoC we assume blittable structs on the C# side. Reference-type
// components (class-based IComponent) are not supported through this store —
// they require GCHandle marshalling and stay on the managed side. See
// docs/NATIVE_CORE.md for the rationale.
class RawComponentStore {
public:
    explicit RawComponentStore(int32_t component_size)
        : component_size_(component_size) {
        if (component_size <= 0) {
            throw std::invalid_argument(
                "RawComponentStore: component_size must be > 0");
        }
    }

    [[nodiscard]] int32_t component_size() const noexcept {
        return component_size_;
    }

    void add(int32_t entity_index, const void* data, int32_t size) {
        if (size != component_size_) {
            throw std::invalid_argument(
                "RawComponentStore: size mismatch on add");
        }
        if (entity_index <= 0) {
            throw std::invalid_argument(
                "RawComponentStore: entity_index must be > 0");
        }

        ensure_sparse_capacity(entity_index);

        int32_t dense_index = sparse_[entity_index];
        if (dense_index != SparseSet<int>::kAbsent) {
            std::memcpy(slot_ptr(dense_index), data,
                        static_cast<std::size_t>(size));
            return;
        }

        const int32_t new_dense_index = count_;
        const std::size_t required_bytes =
            static_cast<std::size_t>((new_dense_index + 1) * component_size_);
        if (required_bytes > dense_bytes_.size()) {
            const std::size_t new_byte_capacity =
                std::max<std::size_t>(required_bytes, dense_bytes_.size() * 2);
            dense_bytes_.resize(new_byte_capacity);
        }
        if (static_cast<std::size_t>(new_dense_index + 1) >
            dense_to_index_.size()) {
            dense_to_index_.resize(dense_to_index_.empty()
                                       ? 16
                                       : dense_to_index_.size() * 2);
        }

        std::memcpy(slot_ptr(new_dense_index), data,
                    static_cast<std::size_t>(size));
        dense_to_index_[new_dense_index] = entity_index;
        sparse_[entity_index] = new_dense_index;
        ++count_;
    }

    [[nodiscard]] bool has(int32_t entity_index) const noexcept {
        if (entity_index <= 0 ||
            entity_index >= static_cast<int32_t>(sparse_.size())) {
            return false;
        }
        return sparse_[entity_index] != SparseSet<int>::kAbsent;
    }

    bool get(int32_t entity_index, void* out_data, int32_t size) const noexcept {
        if (size != component_size_) return false;
        if (!has(entity_index)) return false;
        const int32_t dense_index = sparse_[entity_index];
        std::memcpy(out_data, slot_ptr(dense_index),
                    static_cast<std::size_t>(size));
        return true;
    }

    void remove(int32_t entity_index) noexcept {
        if (entity_index <= 0 ||
            entity_index >= static_cast<int32_t>(sparse_.size())) {
            return;
        }
        const int32_t dense_index_to_remove = sparse_[entity_index];
        if (dense_index_to_remove == SparseSet<int>::kAbsent) return;

        const int32_t last_dense_index = count_ - 1;
        if (dense_index_to_remove != last_dense_index) {
            const int32_t last_entity_index = dense_to_index_[last_dense_index];
            std::memcpy(slot_ptr(dense_index_to_remove),
                        slot_ptr(last_dense_index),
                        static_cast<std::size_t>(component_size_));
            dense_to_index_[dense_index_to_remove] = last_entity_index;
            sparse_[last_entity_index] = dense_index_to_remove;
        }
        sparse_[entity_index] = SparseSet<int>::kAbsent;
        --count_;
    }

    [[nodiscard]] int32_t count() const noexcept { return count_; }

    [[nodiscard]] const std::vector<int32_t>& dense_indices() const noexcept {
        return dense_to_index_;
    }

private:
    uint8_t* slot_ptr(int32_t dense_index) noexcept {
        return dense_bytes_.data() +
               static_cast<std::size_t>(dense_index) *
                   static_cast<std::size_t>(component_size_);
    }
    const uint8_t* slot_ptr(int32_t dense_index) const noexcept {
        return dense_bytes_.data() +
               static_cast<std::size_t>(dense_index) *
                   static_cast<std::size_t>(component_size_);
    }

    void ensure_sparse_capacity(int32_t entity_index) {
        if (entity_index < static_cast<int32_t>(sparse_.size())) return;
        const std::size_t new_size =
            std::max<std::size_t>(
                static_cast<std::size_t>(entity_index) + 1,
                sparse_.empty() ? 256 : sparse_.size() * 2);
        sparse_.resize(new_size, SparseSet<int>::kAbsent);
    }

    int32_t component_size_;
    int32_t count_ = 0;
    std::vector<int32_t> sparse_;
    std::vector<int32_t> dense_to_index_;
    std::vector<uint8_t> dense_bytes_;
};

} // namespace dualfrontier
```

> Observation. `RawComponentStore` re-implements the sparse/dense pattern inline against a `std::vector<uint8_t>` rather than wrapping `SparseSet<T>`. It only borrows `SparseSet<int>::kAbsent` (the sentinel `-1`). The two classes share idioms but not implementation, which is why `SparseSet` ends up unused (§5.4).

### 5.6 `include/world.h`

```cpp
#pragma once

#include <cstdint>
#include <memory>
#include <unordered_map>
#include <vector>

#include "component_store.h"
#include "entity_id.h"

namespace dualfrontier {

// Native mirror of src/DualFrontier.Core/ECS/World.cs.
//
// Intentional simplifications for the PoC:
//   * Not thread-safe. The managed World uses a ConcurrentDictionary for its
//     store map; here we use std::unordered_map because this experiment does
//     not yet exercise the parallel scheduler path.
//   * Deferred destruction is preserved — DestroyEntity only flips the
//     version and queues the id; components are dropped in
//     FlushDestroyedEntities.
//   * Index 0 is reserved as Invalid, same as the managed version.
class World {
public:
    World();

    EntityId create_entity();
    void     destroy_entity(EntityId id);
    [[nodiscard]] bool is_alive(EntityId id) const noexcept;
    [[nodiscard]] int32_t entity_count() const noexcept;
    void     flush_destroyed();

    void add_component(EntityId id, uint32_t type_id, const void* data,
                       int32_t size);
    [[nodiscard]] bool has_component(EntityId id, uint32_t type_id) const noexcept;
    bool get_component(EntityId id, uint32_t type_id, void* out_data,
                       int32_t size) const noexcept;
    void remove_component(EntityId id, uint32_t type_id);
    [[nodiscard]] int32_t component_count(uint32_t type_id) const noexcept;

private:
    static constexpr std::size_t kInitialCapacity = 256;

    RawComponentStore* get_or_create_store(uint32_t type_id, int32_t size);
    const RawComponentStore* find_store(uint32_t type_id) const noexcept;

    std::vector<int32_t> versions_;
    int32_t next_index_ = 1; // Index 0 reserved for Invalid.
    int32_t live_count_ = 0;
    std::vector<int32_t> free_slots_;
    std::vector<EntityId> pending_destroy_;
    std::unordered_map<uint32_t, std::unique_ptr<RawComponentStore>> stores_;
};

} // namespace dualfrontier
```

### 5.7 `src/world.cpp`

```cpp
#include "world.h"

namespace dualfrontier {

World::World() {
    versions_.assign(kInitialCapacity, 0);
}

EntityId World::create_entity() {
    if (!free_slots_.empty()) {
        const int32_t recycled = free_slots_.back();
        free_slots_.pop_back();
        ++live_count_;
        return EntityId{recycled, versions_[recycled]};
    }

    if (static_cast<std::size_t>(next_index_) >= versions_.size()) {
        versions_.resize(versions_.size() * 2, 0);
    }

    const int32_t index = next_index_++;
    ++live_count_;
    return EntityId{index, versions_[index]};
}

bool World::is_alive(EntityId id) const noexcept {
    if (id.index <= 0) return false;
    if (static_cast<std::size_t>(id.index) >= versions_.size()) return false;
    return id.version == versions_[id.index];
}

int32_t World::entity_count() const noexcept {
    return live_count_;
}

void World::destroy_entity(EntityId id) {
    if (!is_alive(id)) return;
    ++versions_[id.index];
    --live_count_;
    pending_destroy_.push_back(id);
}

void World::flush_destroyed() {
    for (const EntityId id : pending_destroy_) {
        for (auto& [type_id, store] : stores_) {
            (void)type_id;
            store->remove(id.index);
        }
        free_slots_.push_back(id.index);
    }
    pending_destroy_.clear();
}

void World::add_component(EntityId id, uint32_t type_id, const void* data,
                          int32_t size) {
    if (!is_alive(id)) return;
    RawComponentStore* store = get_or_create_store(type_id, size);
    store->add(id.index, data, size);
}

bool World::has_component(EntityId id, uint32_t type_id) const noexcept {
    if (!is_alive(id)) return false;
    const RawComponentStore* store = find_store(type_id);
    if (!store) return false;
    return store->has(id.index);
}

bool World::get_component(EntityId id, uint32_t type_id, void* out_data,
                          int32_t size) const noexcept {
    if (!is_alive(id)) return false;
    const RawComponentStore* store = find_store(type_id);
    if (!store) return false;
    return store->get(id.index, out_data, size);
}

void World::remove_component(EntityId id, uint32_t type_id) {
    if (!is_alive(id)) return;
    auto it = stores_.find(type_id);
    if (it == stores_.end()) return;
    it->second->remove(id.index);
}

int32_t World::component_count(uint32_t type_id) const noexcept {
    const RawComponentStore* store = find_store(type_id);
    return store ? store->count() : 0;
}

RawComponentStore* World::get_or_create_store(uint32_t type_id, int32_t size) {
    auto it = stores_.find(type_id);
    if (it != stores_.end()) {
        return it->second.get();
    }
    auto [inserted_it, _] = stores_.emplace(
        type_id, std::make_unique<RawComponentStore>(size));
    return inserted_it->second.get();
}

const RawComponentStore* World::find_store(uint32_t type_id) const noexcept {
    auto it = stores_.find(type_id);
    return it == stores_.end() ? nullptr : it->second.get();
}

} // namespace dualfrontier
```

### 5.8 `src/component_store.cpp` — empty TU

```cpp
#include "component_store.h"

// All functionality is defined inline in component_store.h for the PoC.
// This translation unit exists so the CMake target has a dedicated object
// file for the type-erased store, making future non-inline additions (e.g.
// diagnostic dumps, stable ABI wrappers) straightforward to drop in without
// reshaping the build graph.

namespace dualfrontier {
} // namespace dualfrontier
```

### 5.9 `src/capi.cpp` — extern "C" wrappers

```cpp
#include "df_capi.h"

#include "entity_id.h"
#include "world.h"

using dualfrontier::EntityId;
using dualfrontier::pack_entity;
using dualfrontier::unpack_entity;
using dualfrontier::World;

namespace {

World* as_world(df_world_handle handle) noexcept {
    return static_cast<World*>(handle);
}

} // namespace

extern "C" {

DF_API df_world_handle df_world_create(void) {
    try {
        return new World();
    } catch (...) {
        return nullptr;
    }
}

DF_API void df_world_destroy(df_world_handle world) {
    delete as_world(world);
}

DF_API uint64_t df_world_create_entity(df_world_handle world) {
    if (!world) return 0;
    try {
        return pack_entity(as_world(world)->create_entity());
    } catch (...) {
        return 0;
    }
}

DF_API void df_world_destroy_entity(df_world_handle world, uint64_t entity) {
    if (!world) return;
    as_world(world)->destroy_entity(unpack_entity(entity));
}

DF_API int32_t df_world_is_alive(df_world_handle world, uint64_t entity) {
    if (!world) return 0;
    return as_world(world)->is_alive(unpack_entity(entity)) ? 1 : 0;
}

DF_API int32_t df_world_entity_count(df_world_handle world) {
    if (!world) return 0;
    return as_world(world)->entity_count();
}

DF_API void df_world_flush_destroyed(df_world_handle world) {
    if (!world) return;
    as_world(world)->flush_destroyed();
}

DF_API void df_world_add_component(df_world_handle world,
                                   uint64_t entity,
                                   uint32_t type_id,
                                   const void* data,
                                   int32_t size) {
    if (!world || !data || size <= 0) return;
    try {
        as_world(world)->add_component(unpack_entity(entity), type_id, data,
                                       size);
    } catch (...) {
        // Swallow — the C# side treats Add as void. Diagnostics live in docs.
    }
}

DF_API int32_t df_world_has_component(df_world_handle world,
                                      uint64_t entity,
                                      uint32_t type_id) {
    if (!world) return 0;
    return as_world(world)->has_component(unpack_entity(entity), type_id) ? 1
                                                                          : 0;
}

DF_API int32_t df_world_get_component(df_world_handle world,
                                      uint64_t entity,
                                      uint32_t type_id,
                                      void* out_data,
                                      int32_t size) {
    if (!world || !out_data || size <= 0) return 0;
    return as_world(world)->get_component(unpack_entity(entity), type_id,
                                          out_data, size)
               ? 1
               : 0;
}

DF_API void df_world_remove_component(df_world_handle world,
                                      uint64_t entity,
                                      uint32_t type_id) {
    if (!world) return;
    as_world(world)->remove_component(unpack_entity(entity), type_id);
}

DF_API int32_t df_world_component_count(df_world_handle world,
                                        uint32_t type_id) {
    if (!world) return 0;
    return as_world(world)->component_count(type_id);
}

} // extern "C"
```

### 5.10 `test/selftest.cpp` — standalone validation

```cpp
// Native self-test: exercises the C ABI end-to-end so the library can be
// validated on a machine without a .NET SDK. Prints pass/fail per scenario
// and returns a non-zero exit code on failure.

#include <chrono>
#include <cstdint>
#include <cstdio>
#include <cstring>
#include <vector>

#include "df_capi.h"

namespace {

int g_failures = 0;

#define DF_CHECK(cond, msg)                                                 \
    do {                                                                    \
        if (!(cond)) {                                                      \
            std::printf("  FAIL: %s (line %d)\n", (msg), __LINE__);         \
            ++g_failures;                                                   \
        }                                                                   \
    } while (0)

struct BenchHealth {
    int32_t current;
    int32_t maximum;
};

constexpr uint32_t kHealthTypeId = 0xC0FFEE01u;

void scenario_basic_crud() { /* … see file for body … */ }
void scenario_deferred_destroy() { /* … */ }
void scenario_sparse_set_swap_remove() { /* … */ }
void scenario_throughput() { /* … 100k entities, prints timings + checksum … */ }

} // namespace

int main() {
    std::printf("DualFrontier.Core.Native self-test\n");
    scenario_basic_crud();
    scenario_deferred_destroy();
    scenario_sparse_set_swap_remove();
    scenario_throughput();
    if (g_failures == 0) {
        std::printf("ALL PASSED\n");
        return 0;
    }
    std::printf("%d FAILURE(S)\n", g_failures);
    return 1;
}
```

The four scenarios fully exercise the C ABI: basic CRUD, deferred destroy + slot recycle with stale-handle check, swap-with-last remove correctness, and a 100k-entity throughput probe. Use this file as the executable spec for the C ABI's externally-visible behaviour.

### 5.11 Inferred patterns

| Aspect | Pattern | Evidence |
|---|---|---|
| ECS shape | **Sparse-set** (not archetype, not group-based, not table-based) | `SparseSet<T>` template + `RawComponentStore` |
| Component storage | **Type-erased byte arrays** keyed by `uint32_t type_id` | `RawComponentStore::dense_bytes_` (`std::vector<uint8_t>`) |
| Entity IDs | `(int32 index, int32 version)` packed into `uint64_t` at the boundary | `entity_id.h`, `EntityIdPacking.cs` |
| Version invalidation | Bump version on destroy; `is_alive` is `id.version == versions_[id.index]` | `world.cpp:36`, `world.cpp:26` |
| Slot recycling | Free-list (`std::vector<int32_t> free_slots_`) | `world.cpp:9` |
| Deferred destroy | Two-phase: destroy queues, `flush_destroyed` removes components & frees slots | `world.cpp:36`, `world.cpp:43` |
| Memory ownership | Native owns world + all stores; C# holds an opaque `IntPtr` | `df_world_handle = void*`; `NativeWorld._handle` |
| Memory allocator | Standard library only (`std::vector`, `std::unordered_map`) — no custom allocator | grep |
| Threading | Explicitly **not thread-safe**; comment in `world.h:18-20` calls this out | `world.h:18-20` |
| Error handling | Numeric return codes (`0` failure, `1` success); exceptions caught at the C ABI boundary in `capi.cpp` | `capi.cpp:22-26`, `capi.cpp:71-73` |
| Visibility | `__declspec(dllexport)` on Windows, `__attribute__((visibility("default")))` elsewhere; `-fvisibility=hidden` global | `df_capi.h:6-14`, CMake |
| Calling convention | Default Cdecl on the C ABI side; explicit `CallingConvention.Cdecl` on every P/Invoke | `NativeMethods.cs:25-75` |

The native kernel is an **honest minimal mirror** of the managed `World` / `ComponentStore` pair, with two intentional simplifications stamped in the source comments: no thread-safety, no class-based components.

---

### 6.1 Function table

| Function | Returns | Parameters | Decl |
|---|---|---|---|
| `df_world_create` | `df_world_handle` (= `void*`) | `(void)` | `df_capi.h:44` |
| `df_world_destroy` | `void` | `(df_world_handle world)` | `df_capi.h:45` |
| `df_world_create_entity` | `uint64_t` | `(df_world_handle world)` | `df_capi.h:47` |
| `df_world_destroy_entity` | `void` | `(df_world_handle world, uint64_t entity)` | `df_capi.h:48` |
| `df_world_is_alive` | `int32_t` | `(df_world_handle world, uint64_t entity)` | `df_capi.h:49` |
| `df_world_entity_count` | `int32_t` | `(df_world_handle world)` | `df_capi.h:50` |
| `df_world_flush_destroyed` | `void` | `(df_world_handle world)` | `df_capi.h:51` |
| `df_world_add_component` | `void` | `(df_world_handle world, uint64_t entity, uint32_t type_id, const void* data, int32_t size)` | `df_capi.h:53` |
| `df_world_has_component` | `int32_t` | `(df_world_handle world, uint64_t entity, uint32_t type_id)` | `df_capi.h:60` |
| `df_world_get_component` | `int32_t` | `(df_world_handle world, uint64_t entity, uint32_t type_id, void* out_data, int32_t size)` | `df_capi.h:65` |
| `df_world_remove_component` | `void` | `(df_world_handle world, uint64_t entity, uint32_t type_id)` | `df_capi.h:72` |
| `df_world_component_count` | `int32_t` | `(df_world_handle world, uint32_t type_id)` | `df_capi.h:77` |

**12 functions total. No callbacks. No reverse P/Invoke. No iteration / batch primitives.**

### 6.2 Conventions

| Aspect | Convention |
|---|---|
| Naming | `df_<subject>_<verb>` (`df_world_create_entity`, `df_world_add_component`, …) |
| Calling convention | Cdecl (declared on the C# side; default on the C side) |
| Visibility macro | `DF_API` — `dllexport`/`dllimport` on Windows, `visibility("default")` elsewhere |
| Boolean return | `int32_t` with `0`/`1` (header doc explicitly: `0 = failure / not found`, `1 = success / present`) |
| Out parameters | Caller-allocated buffer + `int32_t size`; native side writes via `memcpy` |
| Error propagation | No `df_get_last_error` and no error codes beyond `0/1`. Out-of-range inputs return `0` rather than crashing (per `df_capi.h:39`). Exceptions caught and translated to "neutral" returns. |
| Resource cleanup | Pair `df_world_create` ↔ `df_world_destroy`; documented as mandatory in `NativeWorld.cs:23` |

### 6.3 Notable absences from the API

These functions are not in `df_capi.h`. Each represents a future increment that the experiment did not reach:

- `df_world_query_*` — no batch query (no `Span<T>` access pattern, no contiguous-iteration primitive). Every read is one P/Invoke per entity.
- No callback registration (`df_world_set_*_callback`) for events.
- No `df_world_clone` / `df_world_serialise` for save/load.
- No iteration helper (`df_world_first_entity`, `df_world_next_entity`, `df_world_for_each`).
- No `df_world_reserve` for capacity hinting.
- No `df_get_last_error` / textual diagnostic surface.

---

## Section 7 — C# bridge layer

The bridge lives in **`src/DualFrontier.Core.Interop/`** — entirely new project (5 files, 343 LOC including the `.csproj`).

### 7.1 `DualFrontier.Core.Interop.csproj`

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <AssemblyName>DualFrontier.Core.Interop</AssemblyName>
    <RootNamespace>DualFrontier.Core.Interop</RootNamespace>
    <AllowUnsafeBlocks>true</AllowUnsafeBlocks>
    <Description>
      Experimental P/Invoke wrappers for the native DualFrontier.Core.Native
      library. Kept as a separate assembly so the managed Core stays
      untouched while the C++ experiment is being evaluated.
    </Description>
  </PropertyGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <ProjectReference Include="..\DualFrontier.Contracts\DualFrontier.Contracts.csproj" />
  </ItemGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <InternalsVisibleTo Include="DualFrontier.Core.Benchmarks" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>
```

> No `<TargetFramework>` declared — relies on `Directory.Build.props` (which sets it for the whole solution) to pick the framework. Confirms the experiment was built and run on the same `net8.0` host as the rest.

### 7.2 `NativeMethods.cs` — flat P/Invoke

```csharp
using System;
using System.Runtime.InteropServices;

namespace DualFrontier.Core.Interop;

internal static class NativeMethods
{
    internal const string DllName = "DualFrontier.Core.Native";

    [DllImport(DllName, CallingConvention = CallingConvention.Cdecl)]
    internal static extern IntPtr df_world_create();

    [DllImport(DllName, CallingConvention = CallingConvention.Cdecl)]
    internal static extern void df_world_destroy(IntPtr world);

    [DllImport(DllName, CallingConvention = CallingConvention.Cdecl)]
    internal static extern ulong df_world_create_entity(IntPtr world);

    [DllImport(DllName, CallingConvention = CallingConvention.Cdecl)]
    internal static extern void df_world_destroy_entity(IntPtr world, ulong entity);

    [DllImport(DllName, CallingConvention = CallingConvention.Cdecl)]
    internal static extern int df_world_is_alive(IntPtr world, ulong entity);

    [DllImport(DllName, CallingConvention = CallingConvention.Cdecl)]
    internal static extern int df_world_entity_count(IntPtr world);

    [DllImport(DllName, CallingConvention = CallingConvention.Cdecl)]
    internal static extern void df_world_flush_destroyed(IntPtr world);

    [DllImport(DllName, CallingConvention = CallingConvention.Cdecl)]
    internal static extern unsafe void df_world_add_component(
        IntPtr world,
        ulong entity,
        uint typeId,
        void* data,
        int size);

    [DllImport(DllName, CallingConvention = CallingConvention.Cdecl)]
    internal static extern int df_world_has_component(
        IntPtr world,
        ulong entity,
        uint typeId);

    [DllImport(DllName, CallingConvention = CallingConvention.Cdecl)]
    internal static extern unsafe int df_world_get_component(
        IntPtr world,
        ulong entity,
        uint typeId,
        void* outData,
        int size);

    [DllImport(DllName, CallingConvention = CallingConvention.Cdecl)]
    internal static extern void df_world_remove_component(
        IntPtr world,
        ulong entity,
        uint typeId);

    [DllImport(DllName, CallingConvention = CallingConvention.Cdecl)]
    internal static extern int df_world_component_count(IntPtr world, uint typeId);
}
```

> Uses **classic `DllImport`** rather than .NET 7+ `LibraryImport` (source-generated marshalling). Probably a deliberate simplicity choice; switching to `LibraryImport` for the hot Add/Get pair would shave a few nanoseconds per call.

### 7.3 `NativeWorld.cs` — managed handle

The managed-side interface is intentionally a near-clone of `DualFrontier.Core.ECS.World`:

```csharp
public sealed class NativeWorld : IDisposable
{
    private IntPtr _handle;

    public NativeWorld() { _handle = NativeMethods.df_world_create(); /* throw on null */ }

    public EntityId CreateEntity();
    public void DestroyEntity(EntityId id);
    public bool IsAlive(EntityId id);
    public int EntityCount { get; }
    public void FlushDestroyedEntities();

    public unsafe void AddComponent<T>(EntityId id, T value) where T : unmanaged;
    public unsafe bool TryGetComponent<T>(EntityId id, out T value) where T : unmanaged;
    public T GetComponent<T>(EntityId id) where T : unmanaged;        // throws on miss
    public bool HasComponent<T>(EntityId id) where T : unmanaged;
    public void RemoveComponent<T>(EntityId id) where T : unmanaged;
    public int GetComponentCount<T>() where T : unmanaged;

    public void Dispose();
    ~NativeWorld() { /* mirror of Dispose, prints nothing */ }
}
```

Key marshalling decisions:
- Generic constraint **`where T : unmanaged`** — class-based `IComponent` cannot pass through. The managed production catalog (still mostly classes) is therefore not directly usable.
- Add path: `&value` taken with `unsafe`, passed through `void*`, native side `memcpy`s `Unsafe.SizeOf<T>()` bytes.
- Get path: caller-stack `T tmp = default;`, `&tmp` passed through, then `value = tmp`.
- Disposal: explicit `Dispose` + finaliser. No `SafeHandle`. A dropped (un-disposed) `NativeWorld` leaks the underlying C++ world (called out in XML doc).
- `_handle == IntPtr.Zero` sentinel for disposed state; every public method checks via `ThrowIfDisposed()`.

### 7.4 `Marshalling/EntityIdPacking.cs`

```csharp
internal static class EntityIdPacking
{
    internal static ulong Pack(EntityId id)
    {
        ulong lo = (uint)id.Index;
        ulong hi = (uint)id.Version;
        return (hi << 32) | lo;
    }

    internal static EntityId Unpack(ulong packed)
    {
        int index = (int)(packed & 0xFFFFFFFFu);
        int version = (int)((packed >> 32) & 0xFFFFFFFFu);
        return new EntityId(index, version);
    }
}
```

Bit-for-bit mirror of `entity_id.h` `pack_entity` / `unpack_entity`. The convention (high = Version, low = Index) is hard-coded in two places — drift between them produces silent miscoding (`docs/NATIVE_CORE.md` §5 already lists this as a risk).

### 7.5 `Marshalling/NativeComponentType.cs` — the type-id machinery

```csharp
internal static class NativeComponentType<T> where T : unmanaged
{
    internal static readonly uint TypeId = ComputeTypeId(typeof(T));
    internal static readonly int Size = Unsafe.SizeOf<T>();

    private static uint ComputeTypeId(Type type)
    {
        string name = type.AssemblyQualifiedName ?? type.FullName ?? type.Name;
        // FNV-1a 32-bit
        const uint offsetBasis = 2166136261u;
        const uint prime = 16777619u;
        uint hash = offsetBasis;
        foreach (char c in name)
        {
            hash ^= c;
            hash *= prime;
        }
        return hash;
    }
}

internal static class NativeComponentTypeRegistry
{
    private static readonly ConcurrentDictionary<uint, Type> _byId = new();

    internal static void Register(uint id, Type type) => _byId[id] = type;
    internal static Type? Lookup(uint id) =>
        _byId.TryGetValue(id, out Type? t) ? t : null;
}
```

Decisions worth flagging:

- The id is a 32-bit FNV-1a hash of `AssemblyQualifiedName`. This is compact and allocation-free (the only allocation is the lookup of the QName itself, which the runtime caches), but it is **not collision-proof**. The native side has a partial detection mechanism — `RawComponentStore::add` throws on size mismatch, so two unrelated 4-byte components hashing to the same id would be rejected; two components with the same byte size would silently corrupt each other.
- The id is cached `static readonly` per generic instantiation, so the FNV-1a cost is amortised to once per process per component type.
- `NativeComponentTypeRegistry` is for diagnostics (e.g. "what's the CLR type behind id `0x4F2E8C1A`?"), not for safety.
- A production replacement is already mapped out in `docs/NATIVE_CORE.md` §7: explicit registry built at app startup so the mapping is auditable.

### 7.6 What the bridge does **not** do

- No batch `AddComponent(Span<EntityId>, Span<T>)` API — every component is one P/Invoke. This caps the achievable speedup (see §10).
- No `Span<T>`-over-native-buffer accessor. There is no way for managed code to read the dense array in place; everything goes through `memcpy`.
- No lifetime helper for `NativeWorld` integration with `IGameServices` / `World` (the production tick loop has no path that touches `NativeWorld`).
- No mod-side or contracts-side awareness — `DualFrontier.Contracts` is unaware that a native world exists; only the benchmark project depends on `DualFrontier.Core.Interop`.

## Section 8 — Test coverage

### 8.1 Native tests

| File | Framework | Scenarios | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| `native/.../test/selftest.cpp` | None — custom `DF_CHECK` macro, prints to stdout, returns non-zero on failure | `scenario_basic_crud` (CRUD round-trip), `scenario_deferred_destroy` (slot recycle, stale-handle invariant), `scenario_sparse_set_swap_remove` (64 entities, alternating remove, payload integrity), `scenario_throughput` (100 000 entities, prints timings + checksum) | Verified in this Discovery, see §10. **ALL PASSED.** |

No third-party C++ test framework (no Catch2, no doctest, no GoogleTest). The choice keeps the build dependency-free.

### 8.2 Managed tests for the bridge — none exist

There is no `DualFrontier.Core.Interop.Tests` project. The C# bridge is exercised exclusively from `DualFrontier.Core.Benchmarks` — and the benchmark code is correctness-by-coincidence: `Program.cs` compares managed and native sums (`Console.Error.WriteLine + Environment.Exit(2)` on mismatch), but BenchmarkDotNet itself does not.

This is a real coverage gap — see §13.

### 8.3 Sample test (excerpt from `selftest.cpp`)

```cpp
void scenario_deferred_destroy() {
    std::printf("scenario_deferred_destroy\n");
    df_world_handle w = df_world_create();
    uint64_t e = df_world_create_entity(w);
    BenchHealth h{10, 10};
    df_world_add_component(w, e, kHealthTypeId, &h, sizeof(h));

    df_world_destroy_entity(w, e);
    DF_CHECK(df_world_is_alive(w, e) == 0,
             "entity not alive immediately after destroy");
    // Before flush, component is still physically present in the store.
    DF_CHECK(df_world_component_count(w, kHealthTypeId) == 1,
             "component still stored pre-flush (deferred removal)");

    df_world_flush_destroyed(w);
    DF_CHECK(df_world_component_count(w, kHealthTypeId) == 0,
             "component removed post-flush");

    // Slot is recycled on the next create; version must bump so the old
    // handle stays stale.
    uint64_t e2 = df_world_create_entity(w);
    DF_CHECK(df_world_is_alive(w, e2) == 1, "recycled slot is alive");
    DF_CHECK(df_world_is_alive(w, e) == 0,
             "stale handle stays dead after recycle");

    df_world_destroy(w);
}
```

This particular scenario encodes the most non-trivial invariants — version bump on destroy, deferred component removal, slot recycling without resurrecting old handles — all of which match the managed `World.cs` semantics.

## Section 9 — Documentation

### 9.1 Markdown files added or substantially modified by the experiment

| Path | Status | Size |
|---|---|---|
| `docs/NATIVE_CORE.md` | Added on this branch only | 205 lines / ~7.5 KB |
| `docs/NATIVE_CORE_EXPERIMENT.md` | Modified — substantial rewrite of decision criterion | 414 lines / ~15 KB |
| `docs/methodology/DEVELOPMENT_HYGIENE.md` | Modified | 313 lines / ~12 KB |
| `native/DualFrontier.Core.Native/build.md` | Added | 72 lines / ~2 KB |

### 9.2 `docs/NATIVE_CORE.md` (full inline)

This is the canonical English doc for the experiment. It covers seven sections: motivation, architectural boundary diagram, P/Invoke mapping table, marshalling option analysis, build/run instructions, decision rule, and a 6-item follow-up list. Already extracted in full in §0 of the prompt context — for reference, the headline points are:

- **Boundary diagram** — `Application → Core (managed Interop layer) → P/Invoke → Native World`. The managed `World` and `NativeWorld` coexist; the experiment does not replace anything.
- **Stays on C# / Ported to C++** tables — `SystemBase`, `SystemExecutionContext`, `DependencyGraph`, `ParallelSystemScheduler`, `EventBus`, `IComponent` marker, components, and `GameServices` all stay on C# in this PoC. `SparseSet<T>`, `RawComponentStore`, and `World` (subset) are ported.
- **Marshalling**: option 1 (blittable struct pass-through) chosen; option 2 (`GCHandle.Alloc(Pinned)`) and option 3 (serialise to `byte[]`) discussed and rejected for the PoC.
- **Decision rule** matrix — `≥ 20% faster on Get and Add combined → continue`, `0–20% → park (try managed structs first)`, `not faster → close`.
- **Risks**: marshalling cost can eat the win; reference-type components require a class→struct refactor across the whole catalog; cross-platform packaging (`runtimes/{rid}/native/...`) is unsolved; mixed-mode debugging is awkward; version drift between `df_capi.h` and `EntityIdPacking.cs` produces silent corruption.
- **Follow-ups** (§7): explicit type-id registry, struct-based component catalog, port `DependencyGraph`, port `ParallelSystemScheduler` (requires reverse P/Invoke for `SystemBase.Update`), `runtimes/{rid}/native/...` packaging, CI matrix.

### 9.3 `docs/NATIVE_CORE_EXPERIMENT.md` — the major rewrite

This document is in Russian and is structurally older (it predates the C++ commits — appears to have been a planning document). The experiment's modifications added §8 ("Критерий принятия решения") which **revises the decision criterion away from mean latency** toward p99, GC pause count, throughput on 2 cores, and long-run drift over 2 hours of simulation.

Key reformulation in §8:

> **Пересмотренный вывод: перенос ядра на C++ оправдан независимо от mean latency бенчмарка.** Основная ценность — предсказуемость и отсутствие GC jitter на длительной симуляции.

Rule table now allows accepting C++ even on mean-latency parity if GC pauses are eliminated and p99 improves on the constrained 2-core target hardware. This is a meaningfully different bar from the English `NATIVE_CORE.md` §6.

> **Inconsistency to flag.** `docs/NATIVE_CORE.md` (English) and `docs/NATIVE_CORE_EXPERIMENT.md` (Russian) define **different decision rules**. NATIVE_CORE.md §6 is "≥20% combined Get+Add → continue"; NATIVE_CORE_EXPERIMENT.md §8 is GC-pause + p99 + long-run-drift on 2-core target. They were authored in adjacent commits (`959ccd0` and `cb65761`), so the inconsistency is deliberate and not yet reconciled. Whichever decision rule Opus is going to apply, this needs resolution first.

### 9.4 `docs/methodology/DEVELOPMENT_HYGIENE.md`

Adds the engine/game boundary checklist for PRs. Pre-emptively names `interop:` and `native:` as legitimate engine-side scope prefixes — i.e. the doc already assumes the C++ branch will land. Includes a 5-item PR checklist (engine doesn't import game; `dotnet build` green with `TreatWarningsAsErrors`; tests don't regress; commits have scope prefixes; commits don't mix engine and game). This doc is **not** strictly experiment code, but the experiment is what motivated adding it.

### 9.5 TODO/FIXME inventory

```bash
grep -rn "TODO\|FIXME\|XXX\|HACK" \
    native/ src/DualFrontier.Core.Interop/ tests/DualFrontier.Core.Benchmarks/ \
    --include="*.cpp" --include="*.h" --include="*.hpp" --include="*.cs" \
    | grep -v "out/build"
```

**Result: empty.** Zero `TODO`/`FIXME`/`XXX`/`HACK` markers in any of the experiment's source files. The documents (`NATIVE_CORE.md`, `NATIVE_CORE_EXPERIMENT.md`) carry the open items instead.

This is unusually clean for a feasibility probe; either the author finished what they intended to ship or chose to keep open items in prose-form follow-up lists rather than embedded comments.

**Result: ALL PASSED on a fresh sandbox build. C ABI behaves as specified, including the deferred-destroy + slot-recycle invariant and the swap-with-last remove correctness.**

### 10.3 Managed build / test — not runnable in this environment

`dotnet` is not installed in the Discovery sandbox. `dotnet build DualFrontier.sln` and `dotnet test` were not executed.

What can be inferred without running them:
- The `.csproj` files for `DualFrontier.Core.Interop` and `DualFrontier.Core.Benchmarks` look syntactically sound.
- `Directory.Build.props` (unchanged on this branch) governs target framework; the BenchmarkDotNet csproj does not override.
- The branch shipped successful BenchmarkDotNet results (see §10.4), proving the managed pipeline ran end-to-end on the original Windows machine on 2026-04-24.
- The branch has 17 SLN projects vs main's 27. If integration is attempted the SLN merge will need to thread the new projects in next to main's grown set.

### 10.4 Committed BenchmarkDotNet results — `BenchmarkDotNet.Artifacts/results/...`

```
BenchmarkDotNet v0.13.12, Windows 11 (10.0.26200.8246)
Unknown processor
.NET SDK 10.0.202
  [Host]   : .NET 8.0.26 (8.0.2626.16921), X64 RyuJIT AVX2
  .NET 8.0 : .NET 8.0.26 (8.0.2626.16921), X64 RyuJIT AVX2

Job=.NET 8.0  Runtime=.NET 8.0
```

| Method            | Mean      | Error    | StdDev    | Median    | Ratio | RatioSD | Gen0    | Gen1    | Gen2    | Allocated | Alloc Ratio |
|-------------------|-----------|----------|-----------|-----------|-------|---------|---------|---------|---------|-----------|-------------|
| ManagedSumCurrent | 101.93 μs | 1.111 μs |  0.928 μs | 101.45 μs |  1.00 |    0.00 |       - |       - |       - |         - |          NA |
| **NativeSumCurrent**  | **95.31 μs**  | 0.470 μs |  0.393 μs |  95.32 μs |  **0.94** |    0.01 |       - |       - |       - |         - |          NA |
| ManagedAdd10k     | 218.24 μs | 4.353 μs | 10.261 μs | 214.22 μs |  2.19 |    0.10 | 83.0078 | 41.5039 | 41.5039 |  655 606 B |          NA |
| **NativeAdd10k**      | **399.83 μs** | 7.951 μs | 19.653 μs | 401.88 μs |  3.92 |    0.13 |       - |       - |       - |       24 B |          NA |

> Note: BenchmarkDotNet's `Ratio` column for `ManagedAdd10k` and `NativeAdd10k` is not a managed↔native comparison — it is each method's ratio against the baseline `ManagedSumCurrent`. The actual Add10k native↔managed ratio is `399.83 / 218.24 ≈ 1.83x slower for native`.

### 10.5 What the actual numbers say

Re-evaluated against the two decision rules in the docs:

| Metric | Native vs Managed | Verdict by `NATIVE_CORE.md §6` (≥20% combined Get+Add → continue) | Verdict by `NATIVE_CORE_EXPERIMENT.md §8` (p99 / GC / drift) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Get (`SumCurrent`, μs) | 95.31 vs 101.93 → **6% faster native** | Below threshold | Encouraging — 6% mean-latency win without GC |
| Add (`Add10k`, μs) | 399.83 vs 218.24 → **83% slower native** | **Hard fail — combined is negative** | Add path is the worst case for P/Invoke (10k transitions, 10k FNV-1a lookups, 10k `memcpy`s); not directly representative of tick-time Add frequency |
| Allocations on Add | 24 B native vs **655 606 B managed** | n/a | **Decisive** — the entire managed Add path triggers Gen0/Gen1/Gen2 collections; native triggers nothing. This is the GC-pause elimination the §8 criterion was designed to capture |
| Allocations on Sum | 0 vs 0 | n/a | Both paths are GC-clean |

**The Add allocation difference is the single most consequential measurement on the page.** Managed Add allocates 655 KB across the 10k iterations; native Add allocates 24 bytes (an outlier from BenchmarkDotNet's own machinery, not from the work itself). For the genre — long-running simulations on weak hardware where GC jitter is the dominant pain — that 27 000× reduction in allocation pressure is the whole point of the C++ path, and is invisible in the mean-latency numbers.

The managed Add allocations come from `Dictionary` resize / `T[]` regrowth in `ComponentStore<T>` — boxing is not the cause (`BenchHealthComponent` is a struct).

### 10.6 What the numbers do **not** say

- **No tick-time benchmark.** The harness measures hot-loop micro-operations, not a full simulation tick. The §8 metrics (p99 tick, long-run drift, 2-core throughput) cannot be read from this table.
- **No GC pause count** for the managed path under sustained load. BenchmarkDotNet's Gen0/1/2 columns show allocation pressure, not actual stop-the-world events.
- **Single-machine.** The numbers are from one Windows 11 box with an "Unknown processor". No data on the 2-core / 8 GB target hardware.
- **No comparison against a struct-converted managed catalog.** The doc explicitly suggests parking C++ if the managed side gets ~50% faster from `class → struct`. That comparison was never run.

The numbers are sufficient to **resist the conclusion that the experiment failed**, but insufficient to **conclude that it succeeded**. They sit exactly where the §8 revised criterion expected them to sit.

## Section 11 — Integration analysis vs current main

### 11.1 The two trees do not share a parent

```bash
$ git merge-base main claude/cpp-core-experiment-cEsyH
$ echo $?
1
```

There is no `merge-base` to rebase from. A literal `git rebase main` will fail without `--root`, and `git merge main` will produce a tree-diff merge spanning ±60 000 lines. **Integration is not a mechanical merge or rebase under any reasonable definition.**

That said — the experiment's actual delta is small (§3.4: ~1 637 LOC concentrated in 4 directories that mostly do not exist on main). The mechanical conflict surface is therefore far smaller than the global tree-diff suggests.

### 11.2 Files the experiment touches that also exist on main

```bash
git diff --name-only $(git merge-base main HEAD)..main   # impossible — no merge-base
```

We can still ask which files the **experiment-relevant subset** changes that **are also present in main**:

| Path | In main? | Branch change | Predicted conflict |
|---|---|---|---|
| `DualFrontier.sln` | Yes (much larger — 27 vs 17 projects) | Adds two project entries (`DualFrontier.Core.Interop`, `DualFrontier.Core.Benchmarks`) | **Mechanical, low risk** — append two `Project(...) ... EndProject` pairs and the configuration block. Main has its own structure; just add the new GUIDs. |
| `src/DualFrontier.Core/DualFrontier.Core.csproj` | Yes | +3 lines: `<InternalsVisibleTo Include="DualFrontier.Core.Benchmarks" />` plus a comment | **None** — main's csproj has the same shape; one-line addition slots in cleanly. |
| `docs/NATIVE_CORE_EXPERIMENT.md` | Yes (already on main with the planning content) | Substantial revision — new §8 decision rule | **Medium** — the branch's version is genuinely newer; needs a manual content merge or a deliberate decision to take the branch's whole text. |
| `docs/methodology/DEVELOPMENT_HYGIENE.md` | Yes (already on main) | Pre-emptive scope-prefix additions for `interop:` / `native:` | **Low** — small addition, slot in. |
| `.gitignore` | Yes | No change in branch (so main's version applies). Branch already ignores `native/*/build/` but **not** `out/`. | **No conflict, but branch carries 39 build-cache files in `out/` that should be removed before integration.** |

### 11.3 Files the experiment adds — no conflict possible

These paths do not exist on main, so adding them is a pure additive operation:

- `native/DualFrontier.Core.Native/**` (10 source files + `CMakeLists.txt` + `build.md`)
- `src/DualFrontier.Core.Interop/**` (4 `.cs` + `.csproj`)
- `tests/DualFrontier.Core.Benchmarks/**` (4 `.cs` + `.csproj` — but **drop the committed `DualFrontier.Core.Native.dll`**)
- `docs/NATIVE_CORE.md`
- `.vscode/settings.json`, `CppProperties.json` (decide before integration whether these belong; the path in `settings.json` is hardcoded Windows)
- `BenchmarkDotNet.Artifacts/**` — should be ignored, not committed (preserve the report file inline in `NATIVE_CORE.md` if needed)

### 11.4 Files in main that the C# bridge would need to be aware of

The branch was authored on top of an older Core. Main's Core has evolved, but its **public ECS API is unchanged** (verified — `git show main:src/DualFrontier.Core/ECS/World.cs` and the branch version both expose the same `CreateEntity`, `DestroyEntity`, `Add/Remove/Has/TryGetComponent<T>` set). The C# diffs in `DualFrontier.Core/ECS/` between the two trees are mostly XML-doc translation (English on branch, Russian on main).

What the bridge depends on, that main provides equivalently:

| Bridge dependency | Provided by main | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `DualFrontier.Contracts.Core.EntityId` | Yes — same shape | The native side assumes `(Index, Version)` packing |
| `DualFrontier.Contracts.Core.IComponent` (marker only) | Yes | Used only to keep `BenchHealthComponent` typed; bridge does not actually need it |
| `DualFrontier.Core.ECS.World` (managed counterpart) | Yes | The benchmark needs it for the comparison |

**The bridge does not depend on anything specific to the branch's older Core snapshot.** Lift-and-shift onto main should not require source modifications inside `DualFrontier.Core.Interop/`.

### 11.5 Main's evolution that the experiment did not see

These are headline main-side changes that landed after the experiment branched off (i.e. on origin/main but not on the local main; the gap matters):

- **`docs/architecture/RUNTIME_ARCHITECTURE.md` v1.0** (origin/main only — `bcd63aa` / `75be805`): the Vulkan migration spec the prompt's own context references. The runtime layer is being designed; the C++ kernel work has not been considered as input to that design.
- The Persistence project (`DualFrontier.Persistence`, `DualFrontier.Persistence.Tests`) — out-of-band of the kernel, but proves the SLN has grown.
- Mod fixture projects — out-of-band.
- Substantial domain content in `Components/`, `Systems/`, `Application/` that the branch has older or absent.

The experiment was authored against an early state of the codebase that **predates Phase 4 / M8 work**. Integration cannot be approached as "fast-forward the branch onto current main" — it has to be **re-applied to current main**, ideally through a clean cherry-pick of the 11 native commits onto a fresh branch from origin/main.

### 11.6 Predicted integration effort by approach

| Approach | Effort | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `git merge` branch into main | **Avoid** — would pull in 140 commits of older codebase as "additions" and reverse-replay 277 main-side changes. Will resolve to enormous conflict set. |
| `git rebase main` (regular) | **Fails** — no merge-base. Without `--root` it cannot start. |
| `git rebase --onto main --root claude/cpp-core-experiment-cEsyH` | **Fails in practice** — replays 151 commits including the 140 unrelated ones. Same conflict pile as a plain merge. |
| **Cherry-pick the 11 C++ commits onto a fresh branch from origin/main** | **2–4 hours** for the mechanical part; conflicts only on `DualFrontier.sln`, `DualFrontier.Core.csproj`, the two doc files. Recommended. |
| **Re-implement on origin/main** (write the 1 600 LOC fresh, using the branch as reference) | **1 day** if the architecture stays the same. Cleaner history, lets you skip the committed `out/` cache, the committed `.dll`, and the BenchmarkDotNet artifacts; lets you fix the path in `.vscode/settings.json` cleanly. The loss is ~2 hours of mechanical typing. |

> **Recommendation (synthesised in §14): cherry-pick `7b5cf78`, `a8d235e`, `cf0eed3`, `6eac732`, `80178c2`, `f59492a`, `e2bc2d9` onto origin/main**, then add cleanup commits. Skip the doc commits (`959ccd0`, `cb65761`, `dd5de10`, `cdeedbb`) — apply those changes fresh, harmonised against `RUNTIME_ARCHITECTURE.md` and against main's existing doc state.

---

## Section 12 — Inferred architectural patterns

### 12.1 ECS pattern

**Sparse-set ECS, type-erased.** Evidence:

- `RawComponentStore` keeps a single `std::vector<uint8_t>` for dense bytes plus a `std::vector<int32_t>` for the sparse → dense mapping (`component_store.h:146-148`).
- Each component type gets its own store, looked up by `uint32_t type_id` (`world.h:52`: `std::unordered_map<uint32_t, std::unique_ptr<RawComponentStore>>`).
- No archetype, no chunked layout, no "groups". An entity that has `{Health, Position}` lives in two unrelated stores.
- The pattern is a strict transliteration of the managed side's `ComponentStore<T>`-per-type model in `src/DualFrontier.Core/ECS/`.

This is **not** Unity DOTS / Bevy archetypes. It is the EnTT default mode (single sparse-set per component) without EnTT's groups. For the Dual Frontier domain — a moderate number of component types, each touching most entities — this is appropriate.

### 12.2 Memory ownership

**Native owns everything below the C ABI; managed owns only an opaque handle.** Evidence:

- `df_world_handle = void*` in `df_capi.h`. `NativeWorld._handle` in C# is `IntPtr`.
- All data (entities, versions, free-slot list, all component byte buffers) lives inside the C++ `World` object (`world.h:47-52`). The C# side never sees a pointer into that data — it always passes a caller-owned buffer for `add`/`get` and lets the native side `memcpy`.
- No `Span<T>` over native memory. No `GCHandle.Alloc`. No shared memory.
- Disposal is one-way: `df_world_destroy` releases everything; managed code holds no resources except the `IntPtr`.

This is the simplest possible ownership model. It pays for that simplicity with a `memcpy` per `Add`/`Get` — which is fine for the current API's per-entity granularity but precludes a `Span<T>`-style hot loop.

### 12.3 Threading model

**Explicitly single-threaded, by design.** Evidence:

- `world.h:18-20`: "Not thread-safe. The managed World uses a ConcurrentDictionary for its store map; here we use std::unordered_map because this experiment does not yet exercise the parallel scheduler path."
- `NativeWorld.cs:21-23`: same warning, same scope — "Not thread-safe."
- No mutex / atomic / TLS use anywhere in the C++ code.
- `ParallelSystemScheduler` (managed) is not aware of `NativeWorld` and therefore never dispatches across it.

The path forward — sketched in `NATIVE_CORE.md` §7 — is to port `DependencyGraph` next (pure algorithm, no CLR dependency), then port the parallel scheduler, then `EventBus`, with reverse P/Invoke for `SystemBase.Update` callbacks. That work is **not started** on this branch.

### 12.4 Component access pattern

**Per-entity P/Invoke, no batching.** Evidence:

- The bridge has `Add/Get/Has/Remove<T>(EntityId)` — every call crosses the boundary.
- No `IEnumerable<T>` over a component type, no `Span<T>` accessor, no view object.
- The Add benchmark proves the cost: 10 000 entities × P/Invoke transition + FNV-1a lookup (cached after first call) + `memcpy` ≈ 400 µs total, i.e. ~40 ns per call. This matches `NATIVE_CORE_EXPERIMENT.md`'s ballpark of "10–50 ns per P/Invoke".

This is the largest design constraint in the bridge. Any system that needs to iterate over components must either (a) repeatedly P/Invoke per entity (the current shape), (b) get a query API added to `df_capi.h` with a contiguous-iteration guarantee, or (c) hold a `Span<T>` pinned over the native dense buffer for the duration of a system's update — which would require the native side to expose pointer + length + a "do not mutate" contract.

### 12.5 System dispatch pattern

**Out of scope for this branch.** Evidence:

- No `df_world_run_system_*` in the C ABI.
- `NativeWorld` has no `Update`-type method.
- The benchmark drives the world directly; there is no scheduler involvement.

### 12.6 Error propagation

**Numeric return codes; exceptions caught at the boundary.** Evidence:

- `int32_t` `0`/`1` returns documented in `df_capi.h:35-39`.
- `capi.cpp:22-26` and `capi.cpp:71-73` show the try/catch pattern at the boundary — out-of-memory or argument-validation throws inside `World` are converted to neutral returns (`nullptr` for create, no-op for void functions, `0` for boolean queries).
- C# bridge maps `0`/`1` to `bool` in `TryGetComponent` etc., then `GetComponent<T>` re-throws as `InvalidOperationException("Component {T} not found")` if the underlying `Try` returned false.
- No `df_get_last_error` and no diagnostic surface — when something goes wrong, you find out via the absence of work, not a message.

### 12.7 Diagram

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ DualFrontier.Application / Systems / Bus (C#)   │
│   uses managed World today; would call          │
│   NativeWorld via Interop in production         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ DualFrontier.Core (C#)                          │
│  - World, ComponentStore<T>, SparseSet<T>       │
│  - DependencyGraph, ParallelSystemScheduler     │
│  - SystemExecutionContext, GameServices         │
│  + InternalsVisibleTo Benchmarks (the 1 line)   │
├──────────────────────────────────────┬──────────┤
│ DualFrontier.Core.Interop (C#) — NEW │          │
│  - NativeWorld (IDisposable)         │          │
│  - NativeMethods (DllImport)         │          │
│  - EntityIdPacking                   │          │
│  - NativeComponentType<T>            │          │
└──────────────────────────────────────┘          │
                       │                          │
                       │ P/Invoke (Cdecl, 12 fns) │
                       ▼                          │
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│ DualFrontier.Core.Native (C++) — NEW         │  │
│  - World                                     │  │
│  - RawComponentStore (type-erased bytes)     │  │
│  - SparseSet<T>  (built but unused — §5.4)   │  │
│  - df_capi.h    (12 extern "C" functions)    │  │
│ Built by CMake — independent of the .sln.    │◄─┘
└──────────────────────────────────────────────┘
                       │
                       └─► Outputs:
                           Win:  DualFrontier.Core.Native.dll
                           Lin:  DualFrontier.Core.Native.so
                           Mac:  DualFrontier.Core.Native.dylib
```
